items.question: Who uses linear regression in practice?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers, economists, and analysts who need a fast interpretable model for continuous targets — price, demand, scores — or a baseline before nonlinear methods.

items.question: What is the linear regression model?
items.answer: A supervised model ŷ = Xw + b (or ŷ = wᵀx + b per example) that predicts continuous y as a linear combination of features plus bias. Training minimizes MSE on labeled (X, y) data.

items.question: When should you choose linear regression over a deep network?
items.answer: When y is continuous, relationships are roughly linear (or linear after feature transforms), interpretability matters, data are limited, or you need a quick OLS baseline before investing in complex models.

items.question: Where does the normal equation appear in the pipeline?
items.answer: At training time as a direct solve w* = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy when X has full column rank and d is moderate — alternative to iterative gradient descent on the same MSE objective.

items.question: Why minimize MSE for standard OLS?
items.answer: MSE is convex in w, penalizes large errors quadratically, has closed-form gradient, and equals Gaussian MLE for homoscedastic noise — see Loss Functions and Maximum Likelihood Estimation for the general MSE definition.

items.question: How do you fit w and b from data?
items.answer: Either solve the normal equation XᵀXw = Xᵀy (with bias column in X), use a stable lstsq/QR solver, or run gradient descent on J(w) = (1/n)Σ(y − Xw)² until convergence.

items.question: Normal equation vs gradient descent for OLS?
items.answer: Both minimize the same MSE. Normal equation is one-shot O(d³) inversion — fine for small d. Gradient descent is iterative O(iter·nd) — preferred for large d, streaming data, or when XᵀX is ill-conditioned.

items.question: Linear regression vs logistic regression?
items.answer: Linear regression: continuous y, ŷ = Xw + b, MSE loss. Logistic regression: discrete classes, sigmoid/softmax probabilities, cross-entropy loss — despite the shared word regression in the second name.

items.question: Cause: duplicate / perfectly collinear feature columns in X. Effect?
items.answer: XᵀX becomes singular; the normal equation has no unique w (infinite weights give the same ŷ). Coefficients are unstable even if predictions on training rows look fine.

items.question: Cause: one extreme outlier in y under pure MSE training. Effect?
items.answer: The fitted line shifts toward the outlier to reduce its large squared error, degrading predictions for typical points — robust or MAE-based losses behave differently.

items.question: What breaks the normal equation w* = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy?
items.answer: Rank-deficient X (collinear features, d > n without regularization) makes XᵀX non-invertible. Numerically near-singular X causes unstable w* even when inv() does not fail outright.
